content:
# Final Review Votes
**Producer:** agent:codex/gpt-5.4

## Summary

All three amendments identify real issues in the daemon success-reconciliation path. One is an operator-noise problem from an unconditional warning on the expected migration path; the other two are correctness bugs in run binding and terminal-lineage repair handling.

## Votes

- `fr-21-bd323524`: Accept
  `persist_workflow_run_id` emits the `eprintln!` whenever it successfully finds and binds a `RunStarted` `run_id` for a task that does not already have `workflow_run_id`. That includes the normal happy path for legacy tasks being retried after this field was introduced. The warning text frames the binding as potentially suspect even when this behavior is expected, so it creates routine stderr noise and unnecessary operator triage. This should be downgraded or made conditional.
- `fr-21-075c3f1b`: Accept
  The binding flow is flawed as described. The claim-time call to `persist_workflow_run_id` scans the journal for the latest historical `RunStarted` and stores that on disk. `mark_active` then re-reads the task, so `active_task.workflow_run_id` now carries that stored value. The post-dispatch call returns immediately because the in-memory task already has `workflow_run_id`, so it never rebinds to the run that just executed. `try_reconcile_success` then uses that persisted id to target a specific `RunStarted` event, which can be the wrong historical run if another run for the same project was started before the retry. That is a real correctness bug, not just a logging concern.
- `fr-21-d1310f41`: Accept
  The daemon path does not fully mirror the CLI’s terminal-sync logic. `update_milestone_state` only switches to `repair_task_run_with_disposition` when it finds an exact terminal lineage match on project, `run_id`, and `started_at`, while the CLI also repairs when a same-`run_id` terminal attempt already exists and computes the effective attempt start from `RunResumed`. That gap matters because resumed attempts can legitimately have a different `started_at`, and `finalize_task_run_internal` rejects non-repair terminal writes when `entry.started_at != started_at`. The existing CLI tests around resumed attempts and same-`run_id` retargeting demonstrate that this is real behavior the daemon currently fails to match.
